examples.explanation: Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0, color 0. colors = [0,-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: DFS(0): neighbors 1,2,3. For neighbor 1: uncolored, set color 1, DFS(1). colors = [0,1,-1,-1]. DFS(1): neighbors 0,2. Neighbor 0 colored 0 (different, ok). Neighbor 2 uncolored, set color 0, DFS(2). colors = [0,1,0,-1]. DFS(2): neighbors 0,1,3. Neighbor 0 colored 0 (same as 2's color 0) → conflict detected. Return False. Graph not bipartite.

Step 1: colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at 0, color 0. colors = [0,-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: DFS(0): neighbors 1,3. Neighbor 1 uncolored, set color 1, DFS(1). colors = [0,1,-1,-1]. DFS(1): neighbors 0,2. Neighbor 0 colored 0 (different). Neighbor 2 uncolored, set color 0, DFS(2). colors = [0,1,0,-1]. DFS(2): neighbors 1,3. Neighbor 1 colored 1 (different). Neighbor 3 uncolored, set color 1, DFS(3). colors = [0,1,0,1]. DFS(3): neighbors 0,2. Both colored and different (0 and 0 vs 1). Step 3: All vertices visited, no conflicts. Return True.

approaches.name: DFS Color Assignment O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Perform DFS traversal while assigning alternating colors (0/1) to adjacent vertices. If any adjacent vertices receive the same color during traversal, the graph is not bipartite. This leverages recursion or stack-based DFS.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph is bipartite using DFS with alternating color assignment.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph is one whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that no two vertices within the same set are adjacent. This problem asks to verify this property for a given undirected graph. The DFS Color Assignment approach works by performing a depth-first traversal while assigning alternating colors (typically 0 and 1) to vertices. Starting from any vertex, assign it color 0. During DFS, for each neighbor, if uncolored, assign the opposite color and recursively process it; if already colored, check if it has the same color as the current vertex—if so, a conflict occurs, indicating the graph is not bipartite. This works because bipartiteness requires all edges to connect vertices of different colors, analogous to a 2-coloring problem. The algorithm must handle disconnected graphs by initiating DFS from each unvisited component. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for the color array and recursion stack. Connection to L1: This full solution integrates the DFS traversal subroutine with color checking logic. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs, forgetting to check for color conflicts before recursing, and incorrect adjacency list representation for undirected edges. Real code pattern: Initialize color array with -1 (uncolored). For each vertex, if uncolored, start DFS with color 0. In DFS, assign color to current vertex, iterate neighbors: if neighbor uncolored, recurse with opposite color; if colored and same as current, return False. If all components succeed, return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at vertex 0, color 0. colors = [0,-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: DFS(0): neighbors 1,2,3. For neighbor 1: uncolored, set color 1, DFS(1). colors = [0,1,-1,-1]. DFS(1): neighbors 0,2. Neighbor 0 colored 0 (different, ok). Neighbor 2 uncolored, set color 0, DFS(2). colors = [0,1,0,-1]. DFS(2): neighbors 0,1,3. Neighbor 0 colored 0 (same as 2's color 0) → conflict detected. Return False. Graph not bipartite.

Step 1: colors = [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Start at 0, color 0. colors = [0,-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: DFS(0): neighbors 1,3. Neighbor 1 uncolored, set color 1, DFS(1). colors = [0,1,-1,-1]. DFS(1): neighbors 0,2. Neighbor 0 colored 0 (different). Neighbor 2 uncolored, set color 0, DFS(2). colors = [0,1,0,-1]. DFS(2): neighbors 1,3. Neighbor 1 colored 1 (different). Neighbor 3 uncolored, set color 1, DFS(3). colors = [0,1,0,1]. DFS(3): neighbors 0,2. Both colored and different (0 and 0 vs 1). Step 3: All vertices visited, no conflicts. Return True.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Color Assignment in Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement DFS to traverse a connected graph component, assigning alternating colors and detecting conflicts.
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the core of the bipartite checking algorithm. It performs a depth-first search from a starting vertex, assigning colors to vertices as it visits them. The intuition is that in a bipartite graph, we can think of colors as representing two sets; edges must always connect vertices of different colors. The DFS ensures we explore all vertices in a component, propagating the color assignment through adjacency relationships. Step-by-step: 1. Assign the given color to the current vertex. 2. For each neighbor: a. If uncolored, recursively call DFS with the opposite color. b. If already colored and same as current color, return False (conflict). 3. If all neighbors processed without conflict, return True. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(V+E) for the component, but in context, it's called per component. Space is O(V) for recursion depth. Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each unvisited vertex in the main function to handle all components. Common mistakes: Not passing the correct opposite color, missing the base case for already visited vertices, or incorrect conflict check. Real code pattern: def dfs(v, color): colors[v] = color; for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor] == -1: if not dfs(neighbor, 1-color): return False; elif colors[neighbor] == color: return False; return True.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors = [-1,-1,-1]. DFS(0,0): set colors[0]=0. Step 2: neighbors 1,2. Neighbor 1: colors[1]==-1, call DFS(1,1). colors[1]=1. DFS(1,1): neighbors 0,2. Neighbor 0: colors[0]=0 (different, ok). Neighbor 2: colors[2]==-1, call DFS(2,0). colors[2]=0. DFS(2,0): neighbors 0,1. Neighbor 0: colors[0]=0 (same as 2's color 0) → conflict. Return False.

Step 1: colors = [-1,-1,-1]. DFS(0,0): colors[0]=0. Step 2: neighbor 1: colors[1]==-1, call DFS(1,1). colors[1]=1. DFS(1,1): neighbors 0,2. Neighbor 0: colors[0]=0 (different). Neighbor 2: colors[2]==-1, call DFS(2,0). colors[2]=0. DFS(2,0): neighbor 1: colors[1]=1 (different). Step 3: All returns True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Color / Group Assignment
ladders.desc: The central technique of assigning opposite colors to adjacent vertices and checking for same-color edges.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the key insight that bipartite checking reduces to a 2-coloring problem: if we can color the graph with two colors such that no adjacent vertices share the same color, it is bipartite. The logic involves maintaining a color array where each vertex is assigned 0 or 1. When processing an edge between vertices u and v, if v is uncolored, assign it the opposite color of u (i.e., 1 - color[u]). If v is already colored, check if color[v] equals color[u]; if equal, a conflict arises because an edge connects vertices of the same color, violating bipartiteness. This works because bipartite graphs have no odd-length cycles; an odd cycle would force two adjacent vertices to have the same color during this assignment. The algorithm propagates colors via DFS or BFS, ensuring all reachable vertices are colored consistently. Time per edge is O(1), contributing to overall O(E). Space is O(V) for the color array. Connection to L1: This logic is embedded within the DFS traversal to handle each neighbor. Common mistakes: Incorrectly computing opposite color (e.g., using 2-color instead of 1-color), or not initializing colors properly. Real code pattern: For edge (u,v): if colors[v] == -1: colors[v] = 1 - colors[u]; else if colors[v] == colors[u]: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: colors[1] is -1 (uncolored). Step 2: Compute opposite color: 1 - colors[0] = 1 - 0 = 1. Step 3: Assign colors[1] = 1. colors becomes [0,1,-1].
Step 1: colors[0] is 0 (colored). Step 2: Check if colors[0] == colors[2]: 0 == 0 → true. Step 3: Conflict, as edge connects two vertices with same color 0.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Represent an undirected graph using adjacency list and perform a simple DFS to visit all vertices.
ladders.explanation: This intermediate concept involves two parts: graph representation and DFS traversal. For bipartite checking, we use an adjacency list: a list of lists where index i contains neighbors of vertex i. For undirected graphs, each edge (u,v) is added to both adjacency[u] and adjacency[v]. This representation allows efficient neighbor access in O(degree) time. DFS traversal is a fundamental graph algorithm that explores as deep as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition is to visit all vertices in a connected component recursively. Steps: 1. Mark current vertex as visited. 2. For each neighbor, if not visited, recursively call DFS. This ensures all vertices are processed. Time complexity is O(V+E) as each vertex and edge is considered once. Space is O(V) for visited array and recursion stack. Connection to L2: This traversal provides the framework to apply color assignment; instead of just marking visited, we assign and check colors. Common mistakes: Incorrect adjacency list construction (missing undirected edges), infinite recursion due to not marking visited, or stack overflow for large graphs. Real code pattern: graph = [[] for _ in range(V)]; for edge in edges: graph[u].append(v); graph[v].append(u). DFS: def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for neighbor in graph[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor).

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize graph = [[],[],[]]. Step 2: Add edge (0,1): graph[0].append(1); graph[1].append(0). graph = [[1],[0],[]]. Step 3: Add edge (1,2): graph[1].append(2); graph[2].append(1). graph = [[1],[0,2],[1]].

Step 1: visited = [False,False,False]. DFS(0): mark visited[0]=True. Step 2: neighbors 1,2. Neighbor 1 not visited, DFS(1): mark visited[1]=True; neighbors 0,2. Neighbor 0 visited, skip; neighbor 2 not visited, DFS(2): mark visited[2]=True; neighbors 0,1 both visited. Step 3: All vertices visited.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration & State Management
ladders.desc: Fundamental operations: accessing neighbors in an adjacency list and updating a color array.
ladders.explanation: This level covers two basic operations essential for the bipartite algorithm. First, iterating over neighbors: given an adjacency list representation (list of lists), for a vertex v, graph[v] returns a list of its adjacent vertices. We iterate through this list using a loop (e.g., for neighbor in graph[v]) to process each edge. This allows efficient traversal of edges incident to v. Second, managing a color array: we use an array colors of size V, initialized to -1 (or another sentinel) to indicate uncolored. During the algorithm, we update colors[v] to 0 or 1 to assign a color, and check colors[neighbor] to see if it's colored. These operations are O(1) per access. The intuition is that we need to track state (color) for each vertex and explore connections. Connection to L3: These operations are used within DFS traversal to implement the logic. Common mistakes: Index out of bounds if vertex indices are incorrect, not initializing the color array properly, or using wrong data types. Real code pattern: colors = [-1]*V; for neighbor in graph[v]: if colors[neighbor] == -1: colors[neighbor] = 1 - colors[v]; else if colors[neighbor] == colors[v]: return False.

examples.trace: Step 1: Access graph[0] = [1,2]. Step 2: Iterate: first neighbor 1, then neighbor 2.
Step 1: colors[0] is -1. Step 2: Set colors[0] = 0. Step 3: Array updated.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Arrays
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, adjacency lists, and arrays for state tracking.
ladders.explanation:
A graph consists of vertices and edges. We represent it using an adjacency list (list of lists). 
We also use arrays (like color[] or parent[]) to track state of each vertex during traversal or union operations. 
This foundation enables all higher-level bipartite checking techniques.

examples.input: Define a graph with vertices 0,1,2 and edges (0,1),(1,2)
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: 0,1,2. Step 2: Edge (0,1): connect 0 and 1. Step 3: Edge (1,2): connect 1 and 2. Step 4: Adjacency: vertex 0 neighbors [1], vertex 1 neighbors [0,2], vertex 2 neighbors [1].

examples.input: Create an array of size 4 initialized to -1
examples.trace: Step 1: Size 4 means indices 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Initialize each element to -1: index 0=-1, 1=-1, 2=-1, 3=-1.

testCases.input: Check if index 2 is -1 in array [-1,-1,-1]

approaches.name: BFS Color Assignment O(V+E)
approaches.summary: Use BFS traversal with a queue, assigning alternating colors level by level. If two connected vertices at the same BFS level or adjacent levels have the same color, the graph is not bipartite.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS
ladders.desc: Complete implementation to determine if an undirected graph is bipartite using BFS traversal with alternating color assignment.
ladders.explanation: A bipartite graph is one whose vertices can be divided into two disjoint sets such that no two vertices within the same set are adjacent. This problem asks us to verify whether a given undirected graph is bipartite. The BFS Color Assignment approach works by performing a breadth-first search traversal of the graph, assigning one of two colors (e.g., 0 and 1) to each vertex as we visit them. The key insight is that in a bipartite graph, all edges must connect vertices of opposite colors. We start BFS from an arbitrary vertex, assign it color 0, then for each neighbor, we assign the opposite color. If we ever encounter a neighbor that already has the same color as the current vertex, we've found an edge connecting two vertices of the same color, which violates the bipartite property, so we return false. If we complete BFS without such conflicts, the graph is bipartite. We must handle disconnected graphs by initiating BFS from each unvisited vertex. The algorithm runs in O(V+E) time since each vertex and edge is processed once, and uses O(V) space for the color array and queue. This approach is efficient and intuitive because BFS naturally explores level by level, making it easy to assign alternating colors: all vertices at the same BFS level should have the same color, and adjacent levels have opposite colors. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected components, forgetting to mark vertices as visited, or incorrectly checking color conflicts. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the BFS traversal with color checking logic to solve the entire problem.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize color array = [-1,-1,-1,-1] for vertices 1-4. Step 2: Start BFS from vertex 1, assign color 0, queue = [1]. Step 3: Process vertex 1, neighbors = [2,4]. Assign color 1 to both (opposite of 0), queue = [2,4]. Step 4: Process vertex 2, color 1, neighbors = [1,3]. Vertex 1 has color 0 (ok), vertex 3 unvisited, assign color 0, queue = [4,3]. Step 5: Process vertex 4, color 1, neighbors = [3,1]. Vertex 3 has color 0 (ok), vertex 1 has color 0 (ok), queue = [3]. Step 6: Process vertex 3, color 0, neighbors = [2,4]. Both have color 
... (simplified)

Step 1: Initialize color array = [-1,-1,-1] for vertices 1-3. Step 2: Start BFS from vertex 1, assign color 0, queue = [1]. Step 3: Process vertex 1, neighbors = [2,3]. Assign color 1 to both, queue = [2,3]. Step 4: Process vertex 2, color 1, neighbors = [1,3]. Vertex 1 has color 0 (ok), vertex 3 has color 1 (conflict: edge 2-3 connects same color), return false.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Color Assignment in Traversal
ladders.desc: Perform BFS on a connected graph component, assigning alternating colors and detecting conflicts.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the core of the bipartite checking algorithm. It takes a starting vertex and performs BFS to traverse all vertices in its connected component. The intuition is that BFS explores vertices level by level, which aligns perfectly with the bipartite requirement: vertices at the same distance from the start should have the same color, and adjacent levels should have opposite colors. We maintain a color array where each vertex is assigned 0 or 1, or -1 if unvisited. Starting from the given vertex, we assign it color 0, enqueue it, and then process the queue. For each dequeued vertex, we examine all its neighbors. If a neighbor is unvisited, we assign it the opposite color and enqueue it. If it's already visited, we check if its color is opposite to the current vertex's color; if not, we've found a conflict and return false immediately. If the BFS completes without conflicts, we return true. The time complexity is O(V+E) for the component, and space is O(V) for the queue and color array. This feeds into L0 by being called for each connected component in the full graph. Common mistakes include not using a queue for BFS, incorrectly updating colors, or missing the conflict check for already visited neighbors. The real code pattern involves a while loop processing the queue and a for loop iterating neighbors.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize colors: [-1,-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: Start BFS at vertex 1, assign color 0, queue=[1]. Step 3: Process vertex 1, neighbors=[2]. Vertex 2 unvisited, assign color 1, queue=[2]. Step 4: Process vertex 2, color 1, neighbors=[1,3]. Vertex 1 has color 0 (ok), vertex 3 unvisited, assign color 0, queue=[3]. Step 5: Process vertex 3, color 0, neighbors=[2,4]. Vertex 2 has color 1 (ok), vertex 4 unvisited, assign color 1, queue=[4]. Step 6: Process vertex 4, color 1, neighbors=[3]. Vertex 3 has color 0 (ok). Queue empty, return true.

Step 1: Initialize colors: [-1,-1,-1]. Step 2: Start BFS at vertex 1, assign color 0, queue=[1]. Step 3: Process vertex 1, neighbors=[2,3]. Both unvisited, assign color 1 to both, queue=[2,3]. Step 4: Process vertex 2, color 1, neighbors=[1,3]. Vertex 1 has color 0 (ok), vertex 3 has color 1 (conflict), return false.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Color / Group Assignment
ladders.desc: The technique of assigning two colors alternately while traversing a graph to check for bipartiteness.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the heart of the bipartite checking algorithm. The intuition is that a graph is bipartite if and only if its vertices can be colored with two colors such that no adjacent vertices share the same color. During traversal (BFS or DFS), we assign colors dynamically: start with an arbitrary vertex and give it color 0. As we move to neighbors, we assign the opposite color (1). This alternation continues: if a vertex has color c, its neighbors get color 1-c. The key check is when we encounter an already visited neighbor: if it has the same color as the current vertex, the graph cannot be bipartite because an edge connects two vertices of the same color. This works because in a bipartite graph, all paths between any two vertices have even length, ensuring color consistency. The algorithm processes each edge once to verify this property. Time complexity is O(E) for the color checks, and space is O(V) for storing colors. This feeds into L1 by being embedded within the BFS traversal. Common mistakes include not initializing colors properly or forgetting to check both visited and unvisited cases. The real code pattern involves a simple conditional: if neighbor is unvisited, assign opposite color; else if colors are same, return false.

examples.trace: Step 1: Assign color 0 to vertex 1. Step 2: Move to neighbor 2, assign opposite color 1. Step 3: From vertex 2, move to neighbor 3, assign opposite color 0. Step 4: From vertex 3, move to neighbor 4, assign opposite color 1. All edges checked: 1-2 (0-1 ok), 2-3 (1-0 ok), 3-4 (0-1 ok). No conflicts, return true.

Step 1: Assign color 0 to vertex 1. Step 2: Move to neighbor 2, assign opposite color 1. Step 3: From vertex 1, move to neighbor 3, assign opposite color 1. Step 4: Check edge 2-3: vertex 2 has color 1, vertex 3 has color 1 (conflict), return false.

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Perform breadth-first search on an undirected graph to visit all vertices in a connected component.
ladders.explanation: BFS is a fundamental graph traversal algorithm that explores vertices level by level, starting from a source vertex. The intuition is to visit all vertices at the current distance before moving to the next level, which is useful for shortest path problems and, in this context, for bipartite checking. We use a queue to manage the order of visitation: enqueue the start vertex, mark it as visited, then repeatedly dequeue a vertex, process it, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This ensures that vertices are visited in increasing order of their distance from the start. For an undirected graph, we represent it as an adjacency list. The time complexity is O(V+E) since each vertex is enqueued once and each edge is examined twice (once from each endpoint). Space complexity is O(V) for the queue and visited array. This feeds into L2 by providing the traversal framework where color assignment is integrated. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited when enqueuing (leading to infinite loops), using a stack instead of a queue, or not handling disconnected graphs. The real code pattern involves initializing a queue, a visited set, and a while loop that processes vertices until the queue is empty.

examples.trace: Step 1: Start at vertex 1, queue=[1], visited={1}. Step 2: Dequeue 1, neighbors=[2,3]. Enqueue 2 and 3, queue=[2,3], visited={1,2,3}. Step 3: Dequeue 2, neighbors=[1,4]. Vertex 1 visited, vertex 4 unvisited, enqueue 4, queue=[3,4], visited={1,2,3,4}. Step 4: Dequeue 3, neighbors=[1,4]. Both visited, queue=[4]. Step 5: Dequeue 4, neighbors=[2,3]. Both visited, queue empty. BFS order is 1,2,3,4.

Step 1: Start at vertex 1, queue=[1], visited={1}. Step 2: Dequeue 1, neighbors=[2]. Enqueue 2, queue=[2], visited={1,2}. Step 3: Dequeue 2, neighbors=[1,3]. Vertex 1 visited, vertex 3 unvisited, enqueue 3, queue=[3], visited={1,2,3}. Step 4: Dequeue 3, neighbors=[2,4]. Vertex 2 visited, vertex 4 unvisited, enqueue 4, queue=[4], visited={1,2,3,4}. Step 5: Dequeue 4, neighbors=[3]. Visited, queue empty. BFS order is 1,2,3,4.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration & State Management
ladders.desc: Implement and use a queue data structure to control the order of vertex visitation in BFS.
ladders.explanation: A queue is a FIFO (First-In, First-Out) data structure essential for BFS. The intuition is that in BFS, we want to visit vertices in the order they are discovered: the first vertex discovered should be the first processed, which matches the queue's behavior. We enqueue the starting vertex, then repeatedly dequeue a vertex, process it, and enqueue its unvisited neighbors. This ensures level-order traversal. In practice, we can use a list or collections.deque in Python for efficiency. The key operations are enqueue (append) and dequeue (pop(0) or popleft). Time complexity for queue operations is O(1) amortized, and space is O(V) for storing vertices in the queue. This feeds into L3 by being the mechanism that drives BFS traversal. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) instead of a queue, which would result in DFS, or not checking if the queue is empty, leading to errors. The real code pattern involves initializing an empty queue, enqueuing the start, and a while loop that continues until the queue is empty, dequeuing and processing each vertex.

examples.trace: Step 1: Enqueue 1, queue=[1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process neighbors [2,3], enqueue 2 and 3, queue=[2,3]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process neighbors [4], enqueue 4, queue=[3,4]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process neighbors [5], enqueue 5, queue=[4,5]. Step 5: Dequeue 4, no unvisited neighbors, queue=[5]. Step 6: Dequeue 5, queue empty.

Step 1: Enqueue 1, queue=[1]. Step 2: Dequeue 1, process neighbors [2], enqueue 2, queue=[2]. Step 3: Dequeue 2, process neighbors [3], enqueue 3, queue=[3]. Step 4: Dequeue 3, process neighbors [4], enqueue 4, queue=[4]. Step 5: Dequeue 4, queue empty.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Arrays
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, adjacency lists, and arrays for state tracking.
ladders.explanation:
A graph consists of vertices and edges. We represent it using an adjacency list (list of lists). 
We also use arrays (like color[] or parent[]) to track state of each vertex during traversal or union operations. 
This foundation enables all higher-level bipartite checking techniques.

examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize list of 5 empty lists (indices 0-4, using 1-indexing). Step 2: For edge [1,2], add 2 to list 1, add 1 to list 2. Step 3: For edge [2,3], add 3 to list 2, add 2 to list 3. Step 4: For edge [3,4], add 4 to list 3, add 3 to list 4. Step 5: For edge [4,1], add 1 to list 4, add 4 to list 1. Final lists: index 0=[], 1=[2,4], 2=[1,3], 3=[2,4], 4=[3,1].

Step 1: Initialize list of 4 empty lists (indices 0-3). Step 2: For edge [1,2], add 2 to list 1, add 1 to list 2. Step 3: For edge [2,3], add 3 to list 2, add 2 to list 3. Step 4: For edge [1,3], add 3 to list 1, add 1 to list 3. Final lists: index 0=[], 1=[2,3], 2=[1,3], 3=[2,1].

approaches.name: Union-Find Bipartite O(V+E α(V))
approaches.summary: Maintain two disjoint sets for bipartite groups using union-find data structure. For each edge, union vertices into opposite groups; if vertices of an edge are already in the same group, the graph is not bipartite.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution using union-find to maintain two disjoint sets for bipartite groups and detect conflicts.
ladders.explanation: This approach leverages the union-find data structure to efficiently check if a graph is bipartite. The core intuition is that in a bipartite graph, vertices can be partitioned into two groups such that all edges connect vertices from different groups. We maintain two disjoint sets: one for each bipartite group. For each edge (u, v), we union u with the opposite group of v (and vice versa). If at any point u and v are already in the same group, a conflict arises, indicating the graph is not bipartite. The algorithm processes all edges in O(E α(V)) time, where α is the inverse Ackermann function, making it nearly linear. Space complexity is O(V) for the union-find structures. This method is efficient and avoids explicit DFS/BFS traversal, relying instead on set operations. Common mistakes include not initializing groups correctly or mishandling union operations for opposite groups. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the key subroutine of union-find with bipartite logic to solve the entire problem. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize union-find structures for V vertices. 2. For each vertex, create a 'mirror' vertex (e.g., vertex + V) to represent its opposite group. 3. For each edge (u, v): union u with mirror(v) and union v with mirror(u). 4. If find(u) == find(v) at any step, return false (not bipartite). 5. After processing all edges, return true (bipartite). Pseudocode: def isBipartite(V, edges): parent = list(range(2*V)), def find(x): while parent[x] != x: parent[x] = parent[parent[x]], x = parent[x], return x, def union(x, y): parent[find(x)] = find(y), for u, v in edges: if find(u) == find(v): return False, union(u, v+V), union(v, u+V), return True. Time: O(V+E α(V)), Space: O(V).

examples.trace:
V=4, edges form a 4-cycle (even length → bipartite).
Initialize parent[0..7] = [0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7].
Process edge (0,1): union(0,5), union(1,4)
Process edge (1,2): union(1,6), union(2,5)
Process edge (2,3): union(2,7), union(3,6)
Process edge (3,0): union(3,4), union(0,7)
No point where find(u) == find(v) for any edge → Return True.

V=3, edges form a triangle (odd cycle → not bipartite).
Process edge (0,1): union(0,4), union(1,3)
Process edge (1,2): union(1,5), union(2,4)
Process edge (2,0): find(2) and find(0) are now in same set → Conflict! Return False.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Color Assignment in Traversal
ladders.desc: Implement union-find to manage two groups per vertex using mirror vertices.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the bipartite checking algorithm. The intuition is to represent each vertex's membership in two possible groups: its original group and its opposite group (mirror). By using union-find, we can efficiently track these relationships. For a vertex u, we assign a mirror vertex u+V (where V is the number of vertices). When we process an edge (u, v), we union u with mirror(v) and v with mirror(u), ensuring they are in opposite groups. If u and v ever end up in the same set, it means they must be in the same group, violating bipartiteness. The algorithm uses path compression and union by rank for efficiency. Time complexity per operation is O(α(V)), and space is O(V) for the parent array. Common mistakes include not initializing mirrors correctly or forgetting to check for conflicts before union. This feeds into L0 by providing the core data structure operations needed for the full solution. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize parent array of size 2*V. 2. Define find(x) with path compression. 3. Define union(x, y) that unions sets of x and y. 4. For each vertex, its mirror is index+V. Pseudocode: parent = list(range(2*V)), def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]), return parent[x], def union(x, y): parent[find(x)] = find(y). This sub-routine is used in L0 to process edges. Time: O(α(V)) per operation, Space: O(V).

examples.input: V=3, initialize and perform unions: union(0,4), union(1,3), union(1,5), union(2,4)
examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3,4,5]. union(0,4): find(0)=0, find(4)=4, set parent[0]=4. Parent: [4,1,2,3,4,5]. union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, set parent[1]=3. Parent: [4,3,2,3,4,5]. union(1,5): find(1)=3, find(5)=5, set parent[3]=5. Parent: [4,3,2,5,4,5]. union(2,4): find(2)=2, find(4)=4, set parent[2]=4. Parent: [4,3,4,5,4,5]. Final parent: [4,3,4,5,4,5].

examples.input: V=2, initialize and perform: union(0,3), union(1,2), check find(0)==find(1)?
examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2,3]. union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=3, set parent[0]=3. Parent: [3,1,2,3]. union(1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=2, set parent[1]=2. Parent: [3,2,2,3]. find(0)=3, find(1)=2, not equal, so False.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Alternating Color / Group Assignment
ladders.desc: The central technique of unioning each vertex with the mirror of its neighbor to enforce bipartite groups.
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essential insight for bipartite checking: to ensure two vertices connected by an edge are in different groups, we union each vertex with the opposite group of the other. For an edge (u, v), we perform union(u, mirror(v)) and union(v, mirror(u)). This operation enforces that u and v cannot be in the same set; if they become so, a conflict is detected. The intuition is that mirror vertices represent the alternative group assignment. By linking u to mirror(v), we assert that if v is in group A, then u must be in group B. Similarly, linking v to mirror(u) ensures consistency. This symmetric operation maintains the bipartite property across the graph. Time complexity is O(α(V)) per edge, and space is O(V) for mirrors. Common mistakes include performing union in only one direction or not using mirrors. This feeds into L1 by providing the specific union pattern used in the sub-routine. Step-by-step: 1. For edge (u, v), compute mirror(u) = u+V, mirror(v) = v+V. 2. Union u with mirror(v). 3. Union v with mirror(u). 4. Check if u and v are in the same set; if yes, conflict. Pseudocode: mirrorU = u + V, mirrorV = v + V, union(u, mirrorV), union(v, mirrorU), if find(u) == find(v): conflict. This logic is applied repeatedly in L1. Time: O(α(V)), Space: O(1) per edge.

examples.trace: Start parent = [0,1,2,3,4,5]. mirror(0)=3, mirror(1)=4. union(0,4): find(0)=0, find(4)=4, set parent[0]=4. Parent: [4,1,2,3,4,5]. union(1,3): find(1)=1, find(3)=3, set parent[1]=3. Parent: [4,3,2,3,4,5]. Check find(0)==find(1)? find(0)=4, find(1)=3, not equal, no conflict.

examples.input: V=2, edge (0,1), parent: [0,1,2,3], after unions check conflict
examples.trace: Start parent = [0,1,2,3]. mirror(0)=2, mirror(1)=3. union(0,3): find(0)=0, find(3)=3, set parent[0]=3. Parent: [3,1,2,3]. union(1,2): find(1)=1, find(2)=2, set parent[1]=2. Parent: [3,2,2,3]. Check find(0)==find(1)? find(0)=3, find(1)=2, not equal, no conflict.

testCases.input: V=1, edge (0,0) not allowed, but test union(0,1), check find(0)==find(0)

ladders.title: Building Block — Graph Traversal Basics
ladders.desc: Implement the union-find data structure with path compression and union by rank.
ladders.explanation: Union-find is a data structure that manages a collection of disjoint sets, supporting two operations: find (determine which set an element belongs to) and union (merge two sets). It's crucial for the bipartite check because it allows efficient grouping of vertices. The intuition is to represent each set as a tree, where the root represents the set identifier. Find operation traverses to the root, using path compression to flatten the tree for future efficiency. Union operation merges two trees, typically by attaching the smaller rank tree under the larger to keep the tree balanced. Time complexity is nearly constant per operation due to inverse Ackermann function. Space complexity is O(n) for parent and rank arrays. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression or union by rank correctly, leading to slower performance. This feeds into L2 by providing the underlying data structure for group assignments. Step-by-step: 1. Initialize parent array where each element is its own parent. 2. Initialize rank array with zeros. 3. find(x): if parent[x] != x, parent[x] = find(parent[x]), return parent[x]. 4. union(x, y): rootX = find(x), rootY = find(y), if rootX != rootY, if rank[rootX] < rank[rootY], parent[rootX] = rootY, else if rank[rootX] > rank[rootY], parent[rootY] = rootX, else parent[rootY] = rootX and rank[rootX] += 1. Pseudocode: parent = list(range(n)), rank = [0]*n, def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]), return parent[x], def union(x, y): rx, ry = find(x), find(y), if rx != ry: if rank[rx] < rank[ry]: parent[rx] = ry, elif rank[rx] > rank[ry]: parent[ry] = rx, else: parent[ry] = rx, rank[rx] += 1. Time: O(α(n)) per operation, Space: O(n).

examples.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(0,2), find(4)
examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, so parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent: [0,0,2,3,4], rank: [1,0,0,0,0]. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, rank[2]=0, rank[3]=0, so parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. Parent: [0,0,2,2,4], rank: [1,0,1,0,0]. union(0,2): find(0)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1, so parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. Parent: [0,0,0,2,4], rank: [2,0,1,0,0]. find(4): parent[4]=4, return 4.

Initialize parent=[0,1,2], rank=[0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0, so parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent: [0,0,2], rank: [1,0,0]. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=0, so parent[2]=0. Parent: [0,0,0], rank: [1,0,0]. find(0): parent[0]=0, return 0.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Neighbor Iteration & State Management
ladders.desc: Implement the find operation that locates the root of a tree and compresses the path.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is fundamental to union-find. Given an element x, it returns the root of the tree representing its set. Path compression optimizes future finds by making each node point directly to the root during the traversal. The intuition is to flatten the tree structure, reducing the height and ensuring near-constant time operations. Step-by-step: start at x, follow parent pointers until reaching the root, then update all visited nodes to point directly to the root. This recursive or iterative process ensures efficiency. Time complexity is O(α(n)) amortized due to compression. Space complexity is O(1) extra per call. Common mistakes include not updating parent pointers correctly or using iterative without compression. This feeds into L3 as a core component of the union-find data structure. Pseudocode: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]), return parent[x]. Alternatively iterative: while parent[x] != x: parent[x] = parent[parent[x]], x = parent[x], return x. Example: if parent = [1,2,2,3] and we call find(0), it updates parent[0] to 2. This operation is used repeatedly in L3 for union and find checks.

examples.trace: Start at 0: parent[0]=1, not root. Recursively find(1): parent[1]=2, not root. find(2): parent[2]=2, root found, return 2. Back to find(1): set parent[1]=2, return 2. Back to find(0): set parent[0]=2, return 2. Final parent: [2,2,2,3].

Start at 3: parent[3]=2, not root. find(2): parent[2]=1, not root. find(1): parent[1]=0, root found, return 0. Back to find(2): set parent[2]=0, return 0. Back to find(3): set parent[3]=0, return 0. Final parent: [0,0,0,0].

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Arrays
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of graphs, adjacency lists, and arrays for state tracking.
ladders.explanation:
A graph consists of vertices and edges. We represent it using an adjacency list (list of lists). 
We also use arrays (like color[] or parent[]) to track state of each vertex during traversal or union operations. 
This foundation enables all higher-level bipartite checking techniques.

examples.input: n=3, parent initialized, find set of 0
examples.trace: Initialize parent = [0,1,2]. For find(0): parent[0]=0, so return 0.

examples.input: n=4, parent set as [1,2,2,3], find set of 0
examples.trace: Parent = [1,2,2,3]. find(0): start at 0, parent[0]=1, move to 1. parent[1]=2, move to 2. parent[2]=2, root found, return 2.
